B:
**Recommendation: Server-side rendering — and specifically, lean toward static generation (SSG) with SSR as the fallback. Avoid pure client-side rendering (CSR).**

For a content-heavy marketing site, this isn't a close call. Here's the reasoning:

## Why server rendering wins here

**SEO is the whole ballgame for marketing.** Marketing sites live or die on organic discovery. With CSR, the initial HTML is essentially an empty `<div id="root">` and crawlers/social scrapers may see nothing until JS executes. Google *can* render JS, but it's slower, inconsistent, and you're gambling your funnel on it. Bing and most social/LLM crawlers are far worse at it. Server-rendered HTML means your content is in the document on first byte — no gamble.

**Social and link previews need HTML meta tags.** Open Graph / Twitter cards are read by scrapers that do **not** run JavaScript. With CSR, your shared links render blank previews — a direct hit to click-through. SSR/SSG puts those tags in the served HTML.

**Faster perceived load = better conversion.** Marketing pages are judged on first impression. Server rendering delivers meaningful content immediately (good LCP/FCP), instead of blank-screen → spinner → content. For content that's mostly read, not interacted with, shipping a CSR JS bundle just to paint text is pure overhead.

**Your content profile fits static perfectly.** "Content-heavy marketing" usually means pages that change on a publish cadence, not per-request. That's the ideal case for **SSG**: build once, serve pre-rendered HTML from a CDN. You get the SEO/speed wins of SSR with cache-everything performance and a smaller attack/ops surface.

## When to deviate

- **SSR over SSG** if content is personalized per request or changes too frequently to rebuild (rare for marketing). ISR / incremental regeneration (Next.js, etc.) is a nice middle ground.
- **Islands / partial hydration** for the few interactive bits (nav, forms, demos) — render static HTML, hydrate only the interactive components. Astro is purpose-built for this; Next.js with Server Components achieves similar.
- **CSR is fine only behind auth** — dashboards, app interiors, anything not meant to rank. That's not your marketing surface.

## Practical pick

Default to a framework that does SSG-first with selective hydration: **Astro** if the site is overwhelmingly content with light interactivity, or **Next.js** (static export / ISR) if you want a single framework spanning marketing + a future app. Either gets you pre-rendered HTML, real meta tags, and CDN-cacheable pages.

**Bottom line:** Render on the server, prefer static generation, and reserve client-side rendering for the genuinely interactive, non-SEO parts.
G:
**Recommendation: Server-side rendering (SSR), or better yet static site generation (SSG) if your content doesn't change per-request.**

For a content-heavy marketing site, render on the server. Here's the reasoning:

**Why SSR/SSG wins here**

- **SEO** — Marketing sites live or die on organic search. Server-rendered HTML arrives fully populated, so crawlers index content reliably without depending on JS execution. CSR ships an empty shell that fills in via JavaScript; indexing is slower and less dependable, especially for non-Google crawlers and link-preview bots (social cards, Slack/iMessage unfurls).
- **First-load performance** — Content is visible on first paint instead of after a JS bundle downloads, parses, and fetches data. Better LCP/FCP, which are ranking signals and directly affect bounce on landing pages.
- **Low interactivity** — Marketing pages are mostly read-only content. CSR's main advantage — rich app-like interactivity without full page reloads — is largely wasted here.
- **Resilience** — Works on slow devices/networks and even with JS disabled or failing.

**SSG vs SSR — pick based on content freshness**

- **SSG (build-time)** — Default choice. Pre-render pages to static HTML, serve from a CDN. Fastest, cheapest, most reliable. Ideal when content updates on a deploy/publish cadence.
- **SSR (request-time)** — Choose only if pages must reflect per-request or frequently-changing data (personalization, live inventory/pricing, A/B logic that can't run at the edge). Add **ISR / incremental regeneration** if you want static speed with periodic content refresh without full rebuilds.

**Practical shape**

Use a framework like Next.js, Astro, or SvelteKit that defaults to server/static rendering and lets you **hydrate only the interactive bits** (forms, menus, carousels). Astro in particular is built for exactly this content-heavy, low-interactivity profile and ships almost no JS by default.

**When CSR would actually be right** — if "marketing site" is really a logged-in app, dashboard, or highly interactive tool behind the landing pages. For the public marketing surface itself, it isn't.

Bottom line: **SSG by default, SSR where content must be request-fresh, CSR only for genuinely app-like sections.**
F:
Use server-side rendering — and more specifically, lean toward static generation (SSG) with selective server rendering for anything dynamic. For a content-heavy marketing site, client-side rendering (CSR) is the wrong default.

Here's the reasoning, in priority order for this kind of site:

**SEO is the deciding factor.** A marketing site lives or dies on organic search. With SSR/SSG, the full HTML content is in the initial response, so crawlers get everything immediately and reliably. CSR ships a near-empty HTML shell and renders content via JavaScript — Google can execute JS, but it does so on a delayed, rationed "second wave," and other crawlers (Bing, social-media link unfurlers, LLM scrapers) handle it poorly or not at all. For content you want ranked and shared, that's a real, measurable cost.

**Time-to-content / perceived speed.** Marketing pages are read, not interacted with. SSR/SSG delivers visible content on the first paint without waiting for a JS bundle to download, parse, and execute. CSR forces the user through that round trip before they see anything — worse Largest Contentful Paint, more bounce, and it disproportionately hurts visitors on slow networks or low-end phones, which is often a chunk of marketing traffic.

**Social sharing and link previews.** Open Graph and Twitter Card scrapers generally don't run JavaScript. With CSR, your shared links render blank or generic previews. SSR/SSG gets these right out of the box.

**The content is mostly static**, so you don't pay for SSR's main downside. Pure SSR (rendering on every request) adds server cost and latency; static generation sidesteps that by building pages at deploy time and serving them from a CDN — fast, cheap, and scales trivially. Use incremental/on-demand regeneration if content updates frequently without a full redeploy.

**Where to make exceptions:** keep client-side rendering for the genuinely interactive, non-indexed pieces — a pricing calculator, a logged-in dashboard, a live chat widget. The standard modern approach is a hybrid: static/SSR for the content and shell, hydrated with client-side interactivity only where it's needed. A framework like Next.js, Astro, or SvelteKit gives you this per-page without an all-or-nothing choice. Astro is especially worth a look for a content-heavy marketing site, since it ships zero JavaScript by default and only hydrates the islands you mark.

The only case I'd reconsider is if "marketing site" really means a web app behind a login with marketing framing — then the interactive surface dominates and the calculus shifts. But for a true content-heavy marketing site, SSG-first with selective SSR is the clear call.
